hero.label: Product Designer
hero.name: Lior Baum
hero.description: I love working on platforms and complex products with awesome people that are passionate about what they're building. I think in systems, I'm savvy with code and AI, and I go just as deep on the details as I do on the big picture.

about.label: About Me
bio: I'm a Product & UX Designer who cares about the full process — from user research to scalable design systems to interfaces that make complex things feel simple.

What sets me apart is where design meets technology. I teach designers at Shenkar to build real products using AI-assisted tools like Cursor and Claude, and I bring that same mindset to my own work. I'm not just delivering Figma files — I prototype in code, move faster, and collaborate with engineers from a place of real understanding.
I thrive in environments where design has actual ownership over the product, not just the pixels.
about.skillsTitle: What Matters to Me as a Designer

experience.description: Improved key parts of the iTero™ scanner experience, focusing on how doctors and patients move through the system. Redesigned navigation and content structure within the iTero™ Store to enhance discoverability and usability. Designed the full UX experience around scanning – from patient onboarding and scan setup to post-scan insights and next steps.

Assisting in a new interdisciplinary course that teaches designers to build functional products using AI-assisted coding tools. Supporting students in translating design concepts into working prototypes using Cursor, Claude, and modern web frameworks. Providing one-on-one mentorship and code reviews, bridging the gap between design thinking and technical implementation.

Led the redesign of the asset management panel within a complex privacy system. Designed flows that supported automated risk detection in sensitive data environments. Partnered with privacy officers and PMs to refine logic and improve system trust and usability.

Reimagined clinician dashboard to reduce friction in digital physical therapy sessions. Conducted interviews with stakeholders and therapists to identify pain points and deliver improved task flows. Created onboarding flows and visual guidance assets to support product adoption.

items.title: iTero Scan Workflow
items.category: Product Design · Redesigning a complex clinical flow.
items.description: Redesigned the end-to-end clinical scan workflow, from setup to review, removing blocking pop-ups and creating a smoother clinical experience.

items.title: Patient Report Redesign
items.category: Product Design · Turning complex data into clarity.
items.description: Rebuilt the patient-report editor with procedure templates, modular content blocks, and image annotation.

items.title: Design System in Claude Code
items.category: Design Systems · Scalable UI built with AI.
items.description: A design system created with AI-assisted workflows and integrated into Storybook, unifying tokens, components, and collaboration rules across web experiences.

items.title: Simplifying Remote Physical Therapy
items.category: Product Design · Streamlining a B2B clinician workflow.
items.description: Redesigned the core daily workflows of a physiotherapy platform, including finding patients, managing care plans, and creating treatment protocols. around one card pattern.

items.title: Graduation Project
paragraphs:
1:1 is an interactive visual diary that turns personal conversations into emotional visualizations. Instead of saving a conversation only as text or audio, the project shows its emotional layer — tone, rhythm, pauses, volume, emotion, and shifts between speakers.

The backend analyzes each conversation, breaks it into segments, and creates structured JSON with data like speaker, timing, emotion, tone, and acoustic qualities.

On the frontend, I used p5.js to create generative ASCII-based visuals. Each conversation becomes a unique visual object made from symbols, density, color, movement, and rhythm.

This was also my first real project using Cursor. It was a big moment for me as a designer, because I could finally build and test the idea by myself, not only design it. It showed me how AI coding tools can give designers more independence and creative power.

The final result is a personal and poetic system for exploring conversations through timeline, people, and emotions.

items.title: ResponsiveView  VS Code Extension
paragraphs:
ResponsiveView is a VS Code extension I created for students in the Vibe Coding course at Shenkar, where I work as a teaching assistant.

The project started from a real need I saw in class: students were building applications for different screen sizes, but testing mobile, desktop, and responsive layouts was not always simple. They had to move between VS Code, browser windows, and DevTools.

To make the process easier, I created a preview tool directly inside VS Code. Students can open their localhost project inside realistic device frames, choose phones, tablets, laptops, desktops, rotate the screen, and test custom breakpoints.

The goal was to help students check their designs faster, understand responsive behavior better, and find layout problems earlier while staying inside their coding environment.

items.title: Techno Site Scrolling Webflow Site
paragraphs:
Techno Archive is a story-scrolling website I created about the history, culture, and visual energy of techno music.

I built the site in Webflow, before vibe coding became part of my workflow, so the process was more manual, experimental, and playful. I used Webflow for the structure, layout, and scroll experience, and added p5.js animations to create dynamic visuals that react to the rhythm and atmosphere of techno.

The project combines storytelling, motion, typography, and generative code. As the user scrolls, the website reveals different moments in techno history through bold visuals, animated patterns, and an immersive dark club-like feeling.

The goal was not only to explain techno, but to make the website feel like techno — repetitive, energetic, visual, and alive.

playground.intro: Between client work, I run small experiments — the bets I make when nobody hands me a brief. Some shipped, some didn't. Every one taught me something.
